conditions: Generalized anxiety symptoms related to new chronic illness diagnosis
medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg every other day
Propranolol 10 mg as needed for palpitations/anxiety (rare use)
health.mental_health: Since the diagnosis last year, she’s been managing health-related anxiety and occasional insomnia, especially around lab results and symptom flare-ups; she’s considering therapy but has only done a few short telehealth sessions.
recent_visits: Endocrinology follow-up in North Austin for TSH/T4 adjustment (6 weeks ago)
Primary care annual physical with lab panel and anemia workup (3 months ago)
Telehealth visit for fatigue and sleep issues (last month)
Lab draw at ARC for thyroid antibodies, ferritin, and vitamin D recheck (2 weeks ago)

finance.debt: Car loan with about $12k remaining; she put several specialist visits and labs on a credit card during a high-deductible period and is paying it down (~$3.5k medical-related balance).
recent_transactions: HSA reimbursement for endocrinology visit and lab work ($420 total over two charges)
Pharmacy copays for levothyroxine and supplements ($28)
Payment plan set up with imaging/lab provider after unexpected deductible charge ($95/month)
Quarterly estimated tax payment for side bookkeeping client ($1,200)

relationships: Partner: Miguel Alvarez (lives together; he helps track medication timing and encourages rest)
Older sister: Priya Reddy (daily texts; Priya is worried and pushes for more aggressive treatment)
Close friend: Jenna Kline (friend from UT Austin days; confidante about anxiety and body changes)
Work friend: Tasha Nguyen (covers for her during lab appointments; knows only limited details)
social.social_media_activity: Mostly passive on Instagram and neighborhood groups; she posts about Austin food spots and hikes but has stopped sharing selfies lately due to feeling self-conscious about weight and fatigue.
private_conversations: Discussing fear of long-term complications and whether symptoms mean medication isn’t working
Talking about libido changes and relationship strain since the diagnosis
Debating whether to disclose her diagnosis at work and how to request flexibility during busy season
Sharing concerns about hair thinning and weight gain and feeling judged

schedule.work_hours: Typically Mon–Fri 8:30am–5:30pm; during tax season (Feb–Apr) she often works 55–65 hours/week with some Saturdays.
regular_appointments: Thyroid labs every 6–8 weeks when adjusting dose (early morning appointments)
Monthly refill reminders and medication timing check-in (self-managed)
Occasional telehealth counseling session (about once a month when she schedules it)
upcoming_events: Early-morning lab draw for TSH/free T4 and ferritin (next Tuesday 7:15am at ARC Far West)
Endocrinology follow-up to review labs and symptoms (in 3 weeks)
CPA CPE webinar on Texas franchise tax updates (Friday afternoon)
Weekend visit to San Antonio to see her sister (in 2 weeks)
schedule.daily_routine: Wakes around 6:30am, takes levothyroxine immediately and waits before coffee; tries to walk 20–30 minutes after work when energy allows; meal-preps simple dinners to keep symptoms stable; aims for lights out by 10:30pm but sleep is inconsistent during flare-ups or busy season.

professional.company: Mid-size public accounting firm in Austin specializing in tech startups and high-net-worth individuals (Cedar Grove Tax & Advisory)
projects: Preparing individual returns for several high-net-worth clients with multi-state filings
Leading a Q1 close support engagement for a Series B SaaS client (revenue recognition and expense accruals)
Implementing a standardized workpaper template and review checklist for the tax team
Advising a client on HSA/FSA compliance and documentation (sensitive due to her own recent learning curve)
professional.performance_issues: Since the diagnosis last year, she’s had intermittent brain fog and fatigue; she’s missed a couple of early-morning meetings after lab appointments and is anxious about making an error during peak deadlines, so she double-checks work more than before, slowing turnaround.
workplace_relationships: Manager: Chris Halpern (supportive but deadline-driven; encourages her to communicate earlier about capacity)
Senior associate: Marisol Garcia (reliable; Daria delegates more review tasks to her during fatigue days)
Client contact: Evan Whitaker (impatient founder; pushes for fast turnaround and increases her stress)

hobbies: Early-morning walks on the Ann and Roy Butler Hike-and-Bike Trail (when energy allows)
Cooking simple high-protein meals and trying anti-inflammatory recipes
Weekend farmers’ market trips (Mueller) for produce
habits: Uses a pill organizer and phone alarms to avoid missing thyroid medication timing
Tracks symptoms (fatigue, heart rate, sleep) in a notes app and occasionally in a wearable dashboard
Limits caffeine and avoids taking iron within a few hours of levothyroxine
Keeps a heating pad at her desk and takes short breaks when she feels wiped out
Occasionally uses OTC sleep aids (melatonin) during high-anxiety weeks
lifestyle.living_situation: Rents a two-bedroom apartment in North Central Austin with her partner; they chose the extra room as a quiet workspace for busy season and a place for her to rest when symptoms flare.
lifestyle.dietary_preferences: Leans toward a Mediterranean-style diet with higher protein and fiber; tries to be consistent with meal timing to reduce energy crashes; moderates alcohol and avoids high-iodine supplements; not strictly gluten-free but sometimes reduces it when symptoms feel worse.
